versions.label: BSB
versions.text: So although in Christ I am bold enough to order you to do what is proper, I prefer to appeal on the basis of love. For I, Paul, am now aged, and a prisoner of Christ Jesus as well. I appeal to you for my child Onesimus, whose father I became while I was in chains. Formerly he was useless to you, but now he has become useful both to you and to me. I am sending back to you him who is my very heart. I would have liked to keep him with me, so that on your behalf he could minister to me in my chains for the gospel. But I did not want to do anything without your consent, so that your goodness will not be out of compulsion, but by your own free will. For perhaps this is why he was separated from you for a while, so that you might have him back for good— no longer as a slave, but better than a slave, as a beloved brother. He is especially beloved to me, but even more so to you, both in person and in the Lord. So if you consider me a partner, receive him as you would receive me. But if he has wronged you in any way or owes you anything, charge it to my account. I, Paul, write this with my own hand. I will repay it—not to mention that you owe me your very self. Yes, brother, let me have some benefit from you in the Lord. Refresh my heart in Christ. Confident of your obedience, I write to you, knowing that you will do even more than I ask. In the meantime, prepare a guest room for me, because I hope that through your prayers I will be restored to you.

context_lines: This is the heart of the letter. Paul is dealing with a real social relationship in the Roman world, but he does so by pressing the implications of the gospel into it. The section moves from apostolic authority to voluntary love, from debt to substitution, and from social status to brotherhood in Christ.
common_interpretation_lines: Paul explicitly says he could **order** Philemon, but chooses instead to **appeal on the basis of love**. That is not weakness. It is the shape of Christian authority rightly exercised.

The center of the passage is verse 16: Onesimus is to be received **"no longer as a slave, but better than a slave, as a beloved brother."** Paul does not treat conversion as a private spiritual layer added on top of unchanged human relationships. He treats union with Christ as something that radically redefines them.

Paul's words in verses 17-19 are also strikingly substitutionary: **"receive him as you would receive me"** and **"charge it to my account."** The logic echoes the gospel itself. Debt is not denied. It is borne.
current_position_lines: This site's current position is that **Philemon 1:8-22** shows the church's relational life must be governed by gospel-shaped brotherhood rather than by the world's status logic. Paul does not ignore the real social arrangement in front of him, but he presses the implications of Christ into it until it can no longer remain untouched.
The passage also shows Christian authority works by truth and love rather than coercive religious power. Paul appeals in a way meant to produce willing obedience shaped by the gospel.

deeper_study.title: The True Church According to Jesus
